The LTL property expresses a safety requirement related to the robot's task completion probability.  It states that if the system is currently calculating the task completion probability (represented by the 'calculateTaskCompletionProbability' atomic proposition) and the probability is lower than in the previous time step (represented by the 'probabilityTaskCompletion_lessThan_previousProbabilityTaskCompletion' atomic proposition), then the system must provide information about variables that contribute to the uncertainty (represented by the 'returnUncertaintyVariables' atomic proposition). The property is always true, if the condition is met.  N_DURATION is set to 2 to indicate that this must hold at the next time step.

explanation: The LTL formula expresses a safety property. It states that it is always the case (G) that if the robot is calculating the task completion probability (calculateTaskCompletionProbability), and the detail is requested (detailRequested) then for the next state (X) of the state, the robot should return the variables contributing to the uncertainty of a calculation (returnUncertaintyVariables). The formula is designed to answer why there is a 90% probability of completing a task, by stating that in the next time step, return the uncertainty variables.

The LTL property expresses that it is always the case that if the robot calculates the task completion probability (calculateTaskCompletionProbability) and also indicates the need for more details (detailRequested), then in the future, the robot should return the variables that influence the uncertainty of the calculation (returnUncertaintyVariables) or the path will be changed.
chosen_LTL_template: ((G ((! (bool_exp1 & (X (! bool_exp1)))) | (X (((! bool_exp1) & (X bool_exp1)) V (bool_exp2 -> bool_exp3))))) & ((! bool_exp1) -> (((! bool_exp1) & (X bool_exp1)) V (bool_exp2 -> bool_exp3))))

explanation: This LTL formula expresses that it is always the case that the robot will return the variables that contribute to the uncertainty of the task completion probability. Also, it captures that the robot must always calculate the task completion probability.  This provides the explanation of the variables affecting the probability and the probability itself, addressing the user's question.
This LTL property expresses that if a task completion status is ever less than the previous probability value, it must be due to the presence of a variable to change in the task.

This LTL property expresses that it is always the case that if the robot is calculating task completion probability, and a detailed explanation is requested, then it should provide information about the variables contributing to the uncertainty of the calculation. The property uses the 'G' (Globally) operator, ensuring this condition holds true throughout the mission. N_DURATION is set to 5.
chosen_LTL_template: ((G ((! ((! bool_exp1) & (X bool_exp1))) | (X ((bool_exp1 & (X (! bool_exp1))) V (bool_exp2 -> bool_exp3))))) & (bool_exp1 -> ((bool_exp1 & (X (! bool_exp1))) V (bool_exp2 -> bool_exp3))))

explanation: This LTL property expresses the requirement that if the robot calculates task completion probability, then it will always provide the variables contributing to the uncertainty of the calculation.

This LTL formula expresses that if the robot calculates the task completion probability (calculateTaskCompletionProbability), then eventually, within a certain duration (N_DURATION), the robot will explain the probability of completing the task (explainTaskProbability) or something else that could stop it from being completed (taskCannotComplete) and where it's providing a detailed explanation (detailRequested). bool_exp1 and bool_exp2 are not used because they don't affect the requirements of the natural language input.

This LTL formula expresses a desire to understand what variables are contributing to the probability of completion and how they may change. The formula looks for a time when the robot calculates task completion probability and then it returns the variables that are contributing to the uncertainty of the calculation. The 'G' (Globally) operator ensures this happens at all points in time. The specific template captures the desired behavior.

This LTL formula captures the user's question by focusing on the variables impacting the task completion probability. It asserts that the robot is tasked with calculating task completion probability, and the LTL property requires the system to return the variables contributing to the uncertainty of a calculation when such calculation occurs in the future.
